facts: Most planets orbit a star, bathed in its light and warmth.
But during violent star system formation, billions of planets are ejected.
These 'rogue planets' drift alone through interstellar space, in endless icy darkness.
Our Milky Way galaxy could host 100 billion to 1 trillion of them – potentially more planets than stars!
idea.payoff: A vast, unseen ocean of dark worlds, silently outnumbering the stars, drifting through the cosmic abyss.
idea.title: Billions of DARK Worlds! 🤯🌌

scenes.visual: Earth rotating in full sunlight. The sun quickly fades to black, leaving Earth dark and cold with distant stars in the background.

scenes.text:
Most planets orbit stars, 
bathed in their warmth and light.
scenes.visual: A vibrant yellow-orange star with 2-3 colorful planets orbiting it, clearly illuminated and warm.

scenes.text:
But during violent star formation, 
billions of planets are ejected.
scenes.visual: A chaotic, turbulent nebula with young stars forming. Planets are depicted being violently slingshotted out from nascent star systems into a dark, empty region of space.

scenes.text:
These 'rogue planets' drift alone 
through interstellar space, 
in endless, icy darkness.
scenes.visual: A single, dark, icy planet slowly tumbles through a vast, starless void. Faint, distant galaxies or nebulae are barely visible.

scenes.text:
Milky Way: 100B - 1T Rogue Planets. 
OUTNUMBERING the stars! 
A vast, unseen ocean of dark worlds.
scenes.visual: A sweeping view of the Milky Way galaxy. Billions of tiny, dark, faint points of light (representing rogue planets) slowly emerge and fill the dark spaces between the stars, clearly outnumbering them.
